title: PEPP Clinical Pearls & Study Hints
description: Key concepts, assessment tips, and clinical pearls from the Pediatric Education for Prehospital Professionals (PEPP) curriculum for exam preparation.

pearls.text: The Pediatric Assessment Triangle (PAT): Appearance + Work of Breathing + Circulation to Skin. Use this 30-second across-the-room assessment to form a general impression before touching the child.
Appearance (TICLS): Tone, Interactability, Consolability, Look/Gaze, Speech/Cry. A child who is not interactive or consolable is critically ill until proven otherwise.
Children are not small adults. Anatomic, physiologic, and developmental differences affect assessment, airway management, medication dosing, and treatment priorities.
Pediatric airway differences: proportionally larger tongue (most common cause of obstruction), more superior/anterior larynx, shorter trachea, smaller diameter airway — all increase obstruction risk.
Positioning the pediatric airway: infants need a shoulder roll to avoid neck flexion (sniffing position). Older children may self-position for maximum airway patency — let them.
Croup (laryngotracheobronchitis): barking cough + inspiratory stridor. Most common viral cause is parainfluenza. Treat with cool mist and nebulized epinephrine if severe.
Epiglottitis: sudden high fever + sore throat + drooling + tripod position + stridor. Do NOT examine the throat or agitate the child. This is a surgical airway emergency — transport immediately.

Respiratory distress signs: increased work of breathing (nasal flaring, retractions, head bobbing, grunting, seesaw respirations). Grunting in an infant = auto-PEEP to maintain alveolar patency — critical sign.
Respiratory failure signs: decreased respiratory rate (exhaustion), cyanosis despite oxygen, decreased mental status, poor muscle tone. Intervene with BVM ventilation immediately.

Asthma is the most common pediatric respiratory emergency. Treat with bronchodilators (albuterol). Severe asthma with silent chest (no wheeze) indicates near-complete obstruction — life-threatening emergency.
Children compensate for shock with tachycardia and increased systemic vascular resistance. Hypotension is a late, pre-arrest sign. Never be reassured by a normal blood pressure alone.
Normal pediatric heart rate varies by age: neonate 120–160 bpm, infant 100–160 bpm, toddler 90–150 bpm, preschool 80–140 bpm, school age 70–120 bpm, adolescent 60–100 bpm.
Capillary refill >2 seconds in children under 6 is abnormal and suggests poor peripheral perfusion. Assess on the sternum or forehead in cold environments for more accurate results.

Pediatric hypovolemic shock: most common cause is dehydration (GI illness), followed by hemorrhage. Clinical signs: tachycardia, pallor, delayed cap refill, decreased urine output, altered mental status.
IV fluid resuscitation in shock: 20 mL/kg normal saline IV bolus, repeated as needed. Reassess after each bolus. Use IO access if IV cannot be established rapidly in a critical child.
Distributive shock in children: most commonly septic shock (look for fever + tachycardia + altered mental status) or anaphylactic shock (urticaria + stridor + hypotension after exposure).
Pediatric GCS: standard GCS is used for children >2 years. For infants, the verbal score uses modified criteria (coos/babbles = 5, irritable cries = 4, cries to pain = 3, moans = 2, none = 1).

Seizures in children: febrile seizures are most common (6 months–5 years, temperature >38°C). Typically brief and self-limiting. Treat the fever. Prolonged seizures (>5 min) = status epilepticus — treat with benzodiazepines.
Altered mental status in a child: AEIOU-TIPS mnemonic — Alcohol, Epilepsy, Insulin (glucose), Opiates, Uremia; Trauma, Infection, Psychiatric, Stroke/Structural. Always check blood glucose.

Pediatric trauma: blunt trauma is more common than penetrating. The large head-to-body ratio makes head injuries more frequent. Solid organs (spleen, liver) are proportionally larger and more exposed.

Non-accidental trauma (NAT/child abuse): suspect when injury pattern is inconsistent with developmental age, history changes, or unexplained injuries (patterned bruising, multiple healing fractures, retinal hemorrhage).
Spinal cord injury without radiographic abnormality (SCIWORA) occurs more commonly in children due to ligamentous laxity. Neurological deficits may be present with normal x-rays.
Pediatric cardiac arrest: most common cause is respiratory failure or shock (asphyxial arrest), not primary cardiac event. High-quality CPR and early ventilation are essential.
Infant CPR: 2-thumb encircling hands technique (2 rescuers) or 2-finger technique (1 rescuer) for chest compressions. Compress at least 1.5 inches (4 cm). Rate: 100–120/min.
Pediatric defibrillation: 2 J/kg for first shock, 4 J/kg for subsequent shocks. Use pediatric pads/attenuator for children <8 years or <25 kg to reduce energy delivery.
Epinephrine 1:10,000 for pediatric cardiac arrest: 0.01 mg/kg IV/IO. For anaphylaxis: epinephrine 1:1,000 IM 0.01 mg/kg (max 0.5 mg). Use the anterolateral thigh in children.

Broselow Tape (pediatric length-based resuscitation tape): estimates weight from length and provides color-coded drug doses and equipment sizes. Use for all pediatric resuscitations when weight is unknown.
Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) in children: Kussmaul respirations, fruity breath, dehydration, nausea/vomiting, elevated glucose. Do NOT correct glucose too rapidly — risk of cerebral edema.

Meningitis in infants: fever + bulging fontanelle + high-pitched cry + photophobia + nuchal rigidity (may be absent in young infants). A petechial/purpuric rash suggests meningococcal disease — true emergency.

Bronchiolitis: most common lower respiratory infection in infants (typically RSV). Presents with wheezing and increased work of breathing in a child <2 years. Treatment is supportive (O2, suction, hydration).
Always get weight (or estimate by Broselow Tape) early — all pediatric drug doses and fluid volumes are weight-based. A mistake in weight can lead to a 10-fold dosing error.
Involve the caregiver: parents and guardians provide critical history and help calm the child. A calm caregiver = a calmer child = a better assessment.
Normal pediatric respiratory rates by age: neonate 40–60/min, infant 30–50/min, toddler 24–40/min, preschool 22–34/min, school age 18–30/min, adolescent 12–16/min.
A child who is too sick to be afraid of you is too sick. A child who is interactive and frightened of you is generally in better shape than one who is listless and indifferent.
Pulse oximetry can be falsely normal in carbon monoxide poisoning. If CO exposure is suspected (enclosed space, fire, altered mental status), give 100% O2 regardless of SpO2 reading.
